body.content:
Kyoto Walking Itineraries - Inside Kyoto
Kyoto is a wonderful city for walking – it’s compact, largely flat, the weather is comfortable most of the year, and drivers are sane. Here are three different one day itineraries for walkers, covering the famous districts of Southern Higashiyama, Northern Higashiyama and Arashiyama.
![Kiyomizudera Temple image copyright Damien Douxchamps](https://photos.smugmug.com/photos/i-JKQ9sXJ/0/L/i-JKQ9sXJ-L.jpg "Kiyomizudera Temple image copyright Damien Douxchamps")
image © [Damien Douxchamps](https://damien.douxchamps.net/photo/japan/kansai/kyoto/higashiyama/kiyomizudera/)
These itineraries are designed to take a half-day to 1 day, depending on the pace at which you want to move and the amount of time you’d like to spend at each stop.
Following each itinerary, I’ve listed some accommodation choice that are convenient to the walk.
Top Activities In Kyoto

##
Check Hotel Availability

Destination

#### Check-out date
![Booking.com](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='150'%20height='0'%20viewBox='0%200%20150%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
### Walking Itinerary 1: [Southern Higashiyama](https://www.insidekyoto.com/southern-higashiyama) Walk
The [Southern Higashiyama District](https://www.insidekyoto.com/southern-higashiyama) is Kyoto’s most famous and most popular sightseeing district, and for good reason: from Shichijo-dori in the south to Sanjo-dori in the north, it’s one long strip of first-rate sights. The itinerary here is described from south to north, but you could just as easily do it in reverse. It can be done in three or four hours, but if you slow down and eat lunch en route, it could take most of a day. Keep in mind that this area will be packed with people during [the cherry blossom season in late March and early April](https://www.insidekyoto.com/when-do-cherry-blossoms-bloom-in-kyoto).
Start by [taking a bus](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-buses) to the Gojozaka bus stop (note that you can also reach this by [taking the Keihan Line](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-trains) to Kiyomizu-Gojo Station and walking east [uphill] on Gojo-dori).
* From Gojozaka stop, walk up Gojozaka and Chawanzaka lanes to [Kiyomizu-dera Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kiyomizu-dera-temple). Take some time to explore the temple, including the Tainai-Meguri, located in front of the main entrance.
* Walk down the scenic [Sannen-zaka and Ninnen-zaka lanes](https://www.insidekyoto.com/ninen-zaka-and-sannen-zaka-preserved-districts). There are plenty of teahouses and restaurants here if you feel like a break.
* At the bottom of Ninnen-zaka, turn right and then left into [Nene-no-Michi](https://www.insidekyoto.com/nene-no-michi-lane), one of Kyoto’s most scenic streets. Consider a short detour into the lovely [Ishibei-Koji lane](https://www.insidekyoto.com/ishibei-koji-lane). Also consider a visit to [Kodai-ji Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kodai-ji-temple), located above Nene-no-Michi (clearly marked).
* Follow Nene-no-Michi north (jogging right then left) to reach [Maruyama-koen Park](https://www.insidekyoto.com/maruyama-koen-park).
* Enjoy [Maruyama-koen Park](https://www.insidekyoto.com/maruyama-koen-park) and then exit the north side of the park and walk to [Chion-in Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/chion-in-temple). You can explore most the temple grounds for free. Walk up the steps through the impressive San-Mon Gate and be sure to go into the main hall (free).
* Leave Chion-in Temple the way you came and turn left (north). A short walk will bring you to [Shoren-in Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/shoren-in-temple) (look out for the giant camphor trees out front). I highly recommend paying to enter Shoren-in – it’s one of the real gems of [Southern Higashiyama](https://www.insidekyoto.com/southern-higashiyama).
* Leave [Shoren-in Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/shoren-in-temple) and walk north to reach Sanjo-dori. Turning left here will bring you to Higashiyama-Sanjo bus stop. Crossing the street and turning left will bring you to Higashiyama Station, which is on the [Tozai Subway Line](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-subways), which will get you [downtown](https://www.insidekyoto.com/downtown-kyoto) and connects to the [Karasuma Subway Line](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-subways) (if you want to get to Kyoto Station). There are a few restaurants near the Sanjo-Jingumichi intersection here where you can grab lunch. Note that if you walk uphill for about 10 minutes, you will find yourself near the start of the Northern Higashiyama Walk outlined below. These two walks can be combined to form a good full-day route.
##### Accommodation Convenient To The Southern Higashiyama Walk:
* [Hyatt Regency Kyoto](https://www.insidekyoto.com/hyatt-regency-kyoto-southern-higashiyama)
* [Westin Miyako Kyoto Hotel](https://www.insidekyoto.com/hotel-the-westin-miyako-northern-higashiyama)
* [Hotel Mume](https://www.insidekyoto.com/hotel-mume-southern-higashiyama)
* [Gion Koyuan](https://www.insidekyoto.com/koyuan)
* [Yuzuya Ryokan](https://www.insidekyoto.com/yuzuya-ryokan)
* [Ryokan Motonago](https://www.insidekyoto.com/motonago-southern-higashiyama)
* [Seikoro](https://www.insidekyoto.com/seikoro-southern-higashiyama)
* [Shiraume](https://www.insidekyoto.com/shiraume-southern-higashiyama)
* [Kyoto Yoshimizu](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-yoshimizu-ryokan-southern-higashiyama)
![Koun-ji subtemple at Nazen-ji Temple image copyright Damien Douxchamps](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='639'%20height='423'%20viewBox='0%200%20639%20423'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Koun-ji subtemple at Nazen-ji Temple image copyright Damien Douxchamps")
image © [Damien Douxchamps](https://damien.douxchamps.net/photo/japan/kansai/kyoto/sakyo/nanzenji/koun-ji/)
### Walking Itinerary 2: Northern Higashiyama Walk
The [Northern Higashiyama area](https://www.insidekyoto.com/northern-higashiyama) is second only to [the Southern Higashiyama area](https://www.insidekyoto.com/southern-higashiyama) as Kyoto’s most popular sightseeing area. In some ways, it’s more pleasant: there’s more greenery and even during busy seasons, it feels less crowded. The route here is described south to north, but you could just as easily do it north to south. It takes three or four hours to do this walk, but if you stop for lunch en route and really take your time, it could take most of a day. Note that the [Path of Philosophy](https://www.insidekyoto.com/the-path-of-philosophy-northern-higashiyama) area will be very crowded during cherry blossom season in late March and early April.
* Start by taking the [Tozai Subway Line](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-subways) to Keage Station. You’ll see a brick-lined tunnel entrance under a grass embankment. Walk through this tunnel and continue straight on for about 200 meters, where you will intersect with an automobile road. Turn right here and go through the main gate of Nanzen-ji Temple.
* Explore the grounds of [Nanzen-ji Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/nanzen-ji-temple-northern-higashiyama) for a while. At the top (east/uphill) end of the grounds, you will find the Hojo Hall, which contains the Leaping Tiger Dragon. This isn’t my favorite garden in town, and the tranquility is spoiled by an annoying tape loop, but if you won’t have time to see other gardens in Kyoto, consider paying to enter. Also note that there are some interesting subtemples up behind the Hojo Hall (reached by going under the aqueduct and following the stream up into the woods).
* Exit the north side of Nanzen-ji Temple complex and take a right, following the road around, past a high school and [Eikan-do Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/eikan-do-temple-northern-higashiyama). After about 200 meters, you will see a sign for the Path of Philosophy (Tetsugaku-no-Michi). Turn right here.
* Walk north on the [Path of Philosophy](https://www.insidekyoto.com/the-path-of-philosophy-northern-higashiyama). This is the pedestrian path that follows the canal north at the base of the Higashiyama Mountains.
* As you near the north end of the Path of Philosophy, you will see a few temples marked off of the path. Consider taking a detour to visit the wonderful (and free) [Honen-in Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/honen-in-temple-northern-higashiyama).
* Continue up the Path of Philosophy until you reach a bridge and a busy temple-approach street lined with shops. Take a right here and you will reach the wildly popular Ginkaku-ji Temple.
* Visit [Ginkaku-ji Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/ginkaku-ji-temple-northern-higashiyama), being sure to take the footpath at the base of the mountains to the viewpoint over the garden.
* Return to the temple approach street and follow Ginkaku-ji Michi (which parallels the canal) west to reach Shirakawa-dori. Cross Shirakawa-dori and you will find Ginkakuji-michi bus stop about 50 meters west on Imadegawa-dori. Here, [you can catch buses to all parts of Kyoto](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-buses), including Kyoto Station.
##### Accommodation Convenient To The Northern Higashiyama Walk:
* [Kyoto Garden Ryokan Yachiyo Hotel](https://www.insidekyoto.com/garden-ryokan-yachiyo)
* [Westin Miyako Hotel Kyoto](https://www.insidekyoto.com/hotel-the-westin-miyako-northern-higashiyama)
![Arashiyama Bamboo Grove image copyright Damien Douxchamps](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='650'%20height='435'%20viewBox='0%200%20650%20435'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Arashiyama Bamboo Grove image copyright Damien Douxchamps")
image © [Damien Douxchamps](https://damien.douxchamps.net/photo/japan/kansai/kyoto/arashiyama/takenomichi/)
### Walking Itinerary 3: Arashiyama
Arashiyama is the next main sightseeing area in Kyoto after [Southern Higashiyama](https://www.insidekyoto.com/southern-higashiyama) and [Northern Higashiyama](https://www.insidekyoto.com/northern-higashiyama). It’s very popular with Japanese sightseers and it gets crowded in [the March/April cherry blossom season](https://www.insidekyoto.com/when-do-cherry-blossoms-bloom-in-kyoto) and [the November fall foliage season](https://www.insidekyoto.com/autumn-colors-in-kyoto). The itinerary here is described from south to north. Keep in mind that there aren’t many places to eat en route, so either eat before starting or bring some snacks. The route takes about four hours to do, but could easily extend to six hours if you take your time.
* Get yourself to Arashiyama (see the [Arashiyama District page](https://www.insidekyoto.com/arashiyama) for transport details).
* Enter the main gate of the superb [Tenryu-ji Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/tenryu-ji-temple-arashiyama) and explore the temple (both the inside of the hall and the outside garden areas).
* Walk uphill and through the garden to reach the north exit of Tenryu-ji Temple. Take a left outside the exit and walk uphill and you will find yourself inside [the incredible Arashiyama Bamboo Grove](https://www.insidekyoto.com/arashiyama-bamboo-grove).
* Walk uphill through the Arashiyama Bamboo Grove and when you reach the top of the hill and the road, look across the road and to the right a bit and you will see a small hut. This is the ticket booth for the sublime Okochi-Sanso Villa. Pay the admission fee and walk up into the villa grounds.
* Take your time exploring [Okochi-Sanso Villa](https://www.insidekyoto.com/okochi-sanso-villa-arashiyama) and don’t forget to stop in the rest house after exploring the villa to enjoy a cup of matcha tea and a sweet (included in the price of admission, so don’t lose the tea ticket).
* Leave Okochi-Sanso Villa and take a left onto the main sightseeing road, heading north. Go downhill and pass a pond on your left. The route here goes past a string of temples: [Jojakko-ji Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/jojakko-ji-temple-arashiyama), [Nison-in Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/nison-in-temple), Takiguchi-dera Temple and [Gio-ji Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/gio-ji-temple). Soon after passing Jojakko-ji Temple, you’ll see the quaint thatched-roof [Rakushisha Poet’s hut](https://www.insidekyoto.com/rakushisha-poets-hut-arashiyama), which belonged to a disciple of the famed haiku poet Matsuo Basho. While it’s tempting to visit every temple on the route, it would take a long time and cost a fair bit of money. Thus, I usually visit only Gio-ji along this stretch.
* Visit [Gio-ji Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/gio-ji-temple) (you might have to ask someone for directions, as it’s a little difficult to spot). After visiting the temple, return to the main route and continue northwest.
* You’ll pass several shops and a few restaurants. After about 250 meters, you’ll see the steps leading up to [Adashino-Nembutsu-ji Temple](https://www.insidekyoto.com/adashino-nembutsu-ji-temple) on your left. Consider visiting this temple.
* Walk north from Adashino-Nembutsu-ji Temple and you’ll soon get to the bright orange [Atago Torii](https://www.insidekyoto.com/atago-torii-arashiyama), a lovely Shinto shrine gate. Near here, you’ll see the thatched roof of Ayu Cha Hiranoya, which serves matcha tea and a sweet for ¥800.
* You can either retrace your stops all the way back to central Arashiyama here, or you can walk up the stone steps on the left, just before the highway overpass, to [catch bus No 62 or 72 back to Arashiyama](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-buses) or onward to Kyoto station.
##### Accommodation Convenient To The Arashiyama Walk:
* [Hoshinoya Kyoto](https://www.insidekyoto.com/hoshinoya-kyoto-arashiyama)
* [Hotel Granvia Kyoto](https://www.insidekyoto.com/hotel-granvia-kyoto-kyoto-station-area)
(not in Arashiyama, but close to transport that leads directly to Arashiyama)
### Browse All Kyoto Itineraries
* [Must See Kyoto - 1 Day Itinerary](https://www.insidekyoto.com/must-see-kyoto-1-day-itinerary)

* [Kyoto Itineraries](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-itineraries) - How To Spend 1 to 5 Days In Kyoto
### Kyoto Vacation Checklist
* [ESSENTIALS](https://www.insidekyoto.com/first-time-in-kyoto?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=IK-Checklist-01) – For all the essentials in a brief overview, see my [First Time In Kyoto](https://www.insidekyoto.com/first-time-in-kyoto?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=IK-Checklist-02) guide
* [HOTELS](https://www.booking.com/city/jp/kyoto.html?aid=965263;label=IK-Checklist-03) – Check Kyoto accommodation availability on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/city/jp/kyoto.html?aid=965263;label=IK-Checklist-04) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?pcs=1&cid=1592349&city=1784&tag=IK-Checklist-05) - often you can book with no upfront payment and free cancellation
* [BULLET TRAIN](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=826984&k_site=https://www.klook.com/en-US/activity/81022-japan-rails/) – You can buy [shinkansen (bullet train) tickets online](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=826984&k_site=https://www.klook.com/en-US/activity/81022-japan-rails/) from Klook - popular routes include [Tokyo to Kyoto](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=826982&k_site=https://www.klook.com/rails-32/1012-japan/route-1305/tokyo-to-kyoto-trains/), [Kyoto to Osaka](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=826982&k_site=https://www.klook.com/rails-32/1012-japan/route-1474/kyoto-to-osaka-trains/) and [Kyoto to Tokyo](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=826982&k_site=https://www.klook.com/rails-32/1012-japan/route-1307/kyoto-to-tokyo-trains/)
* [WHERE TO STAY](https://www.insidekyoto.com/where-to-stay-in-kyoto?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=IK-Checklist-06) – Need tips on where to stay? See my one page guide [Where To Stay In Kyoto](https://www.insidekyoto.com/where-to-stay-in-kyoto?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=IK-Checklist-07)
* [PACKING LIST](https://www.insidekyoto.com/packing-list-for-japan?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=IK-Checklist-08) – See my comprehensive [Packing List For Japan](https://www.insidekyoto.com/packing-list-for-japan?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=IK-Checklist-09)
* [SIM CARDS](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=2652&aff_adid=877200&k_site=https://www.klook.com/search/result/?query=japan%20esim) – You can buy an [eSim to activate in Japan](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=877279&k_site=https://www.klook.com/search/result/?query=japan%20esim) or buy a data-only SIM card online for collection when you arrive at [Tokyo's Narita or Haneda Airports](<https://www.getyourguide.com/s/?q=tokyo sim&searchSource=8&partner_id=LAMDN2W&utm_medium=online_publisher&placement=content-middle&cmp=IK-Checklist-10>) or [Kansai International Airport](<https://www.getyourguide.com/s/?q=kansai sim&searchSource=8&partner_id=LAMDN2W&utm_medium=online_publisher&placement=content-middle&cmp=IK-Checklist-11>). You can also rent an [unlimited data pocket wifi router](<https://www.getyourguide.com/s/?q=japan wifi&searchSource=7&partner_id=LAMDN2W&utm_medium=online_publisher&placement=content-middle&cmp=IK-Checklist-12>)
* [FLIGHTS](https://www.skyscanner.net/g/referrals/v1/flights/cheap-flights-to?adId=1100662&campaignId=13416&destination=tyoa&mediaPartnerId=1924024) – Compare Japan flight prices and timings to [find the best deals](https://www.skyscanner.net/g/referrals/v1/flights/cheap-flights-to?adId=1100662&campaignId=13416&destination=tyoa&mediaPartnerId=1924024)
* [JAPAN RAIL PASS](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=616549&k_site=https://www.klook.com/activity/1420-7-day-whole-japan-rail-pass-jr-pass/) – If you're making frequent train journeys during your visit, you might save money with [Japan Rail Pass](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=616549&k_site=https://www.klook.com/activity/1420-7-day-whole-japan-rail-pass-jr-pass/) – see [if it's worth it](https://www.insidekyoto.com/japan-rail-pass-is-it-worth-it?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=IK-Checklist-13) for you
* [TRAVEL INSURANCE](https://www.tkqlhce.com/click-7919142-15371527) – [World Nomads](https://www.tkqlhce.com/click-7647395-15371527) offers simple and flexible travel insurance. Buy at home or while traveling and claim online from anywhere in the world
* [WANT HELP?](https://www.insidekyoto.com/japan-travel-consulting?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=IK-Checklist-14) – Do you want help planning your trip? Chris Rowthorn and his team of Japan experts at [Japan Travel Consulting](https://www.insidekyoto.com/japan-travel-consulting?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=IK-Checklist-15) can help

### About InsideKyoto.com and Chris Rowthorn
* Chris Rowthorn is the co-founder of [InsideKyoto.com](https://www.insidekyoto.com) and has an unparalleled travel knowledge of Kyoto spanning three decades. Chris has written or commissioned and edited every page on the Inside Kyoto website.
* A former author of **Lonely Planet Kyoto** and **Lonely Planet Japan** guidebooks, Chris is also the director of the Kyoto walking tour company [Chris Rowthorn Tours](http://www.chrisrowthorn.com), and [Japan Travel Consulting](https://insideosaka.com/japan-travel-consulting/), where you can get bespoke travel advice for your Japan trip.
* [InsideKyoto.com](https://www.insidekyoto.com) has been cited by the **[New York Times](https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/17/travel/booking-hotels-in-asia-the-getaway.html)** and **[The Times of London](https://www.thetimes.com/article/926c41d6-bb7d-11e5-8ae7-f2021079f4dd)**, among many other publications and websites.
